title: Graph Traversal Step-Through — DFS vs. BFS
description: Given the same 10-node directed graph rendered twice, the learner can step both a depth-first and a breadth-first traversal from the same start node one click at a time and correctly predict which node is visited next at each step, explaining why the two orders diverge at the first branching node.
creator: Learning Graphs
author: Dan McCreary
subject: Learning Graphs
chapter_title: Graph Theory: Paths, Traversal, and Connectivity

educational.topic: Graph Traversal, Depth-First Search, Breadth-First Search
learningObjectives: Step a depth-first and a breadth-first traversal through the same directed graph one visit at a time
Predict which node each algorithm will visit next before revealing it
Explain why the two visit orders diverge immediately after the first branching node
Describe what backtracking looks like in DFS and why BFS never backtracks

keyQuestions: Both traversals start at A and both visit B second — so why do they disagree at step 3?
Which algorithm reaches the shared sink node J first, and what does that tell you about what each one optimizes for?
If you wanted to find the deepest prerequisite chain under a concept, which traversal would you reach for?
If you wanted the concepts closest to what a learner already knows, which traversal answers that?
commonMisconceptions: That DFS and BFS visit different sets of nodes — they visit exactly the same reachable set, only in a different order
That the traversals diverge at the branching node itself — both still visit B second; the divergence shows up on the step after
That a node can be visited twice if two paths lead to it — the visited-set is what prevents this and what keeps traversal terminating
assessmentOpportunities: Before each click, ask learners to write down which node each panel will visit next
Ask learners to predict the visit order from a start node other than A, then verify with the dropdown
Ask learners to explain why node J is visited 4th by DFS but 10th by BFS
